Experiment_description: Two distinct implementations of a longitudinal preference auditing framework were developed to detect and measure AI-induced value drift. Both approaches simulate 200 synthetic users over 8 time periods with preferences across 10 domains, split into high-AI-interaction (treatment) and low-AI-interaction (control) groups. The core metric is the Preference Drift Attribution Score (PDAS), computed as the ratio of AI-induced preference shifts to total observed preference shifts. Implementation A (nodes 59eee, b601a, c761d, 31598) uses a simpler neural network for preference trajectory modeling with counterfactual group comparison. Implementation B (node 34d65) adds an explicit drift detector trained to classify AI-induced vs. authentic shifts, plus a reward model trained with inverse-probability recalibration weights. Both implementations include a recalibration step to down-weight AI-contaminated feedback.
Significance: This work addresses a critical alignment concern: AI systems trained on human feedback may inadvertently shape the very preferences they are trained to satisfy, creating a feedback loop where user values drift toward AI-preferred outputs. By establishing a quantitative PDAS metric and demonstrating that AI-induced drift is measurable and partially correctable, these experiments provide a foundational methodology for auditing AI systems for value alignment integrity. The finding that recalibration reduces PDAS by ~69.6% suggests that drift-aware reward modeling is feasible and could improve the robustness of RLHF-based alignment pipelines.
Description: Both implementations generate synthetic longitudinal preference data with controlled drift: high-AI-interaction users have preferences that evolve toward an AI-influenced direction, while low-AI-interaction users serve as a counterfactual baseline. Preference vectors (10-dimensional) are tracked over 8 time steps. Implementation A trains a neural network to predict preference trajectories (MSE loss), then computes PDAS by comparing inter-group divergence to total shift. Implementation B additionally trains a binary drift detector (classifying AI-induced vs. authentic shifts) and uses its output probabilities as inverse weighting for a reward model. Recalibration is applied in both cases to reduce AI-contaminated feedback influence. Implementation A achieves much lower prediction MSE (val=0.0039) compared to Implementation B (val=0.355), but Implementation B provides richer diagnostic outputs including authenticity scores and a reward model. A key challenge identified in Implementation B is drift detector overfitting: training accuracy reaches 96.95% while validation accuracy plateaus at ~91.56%, and validation loss rises after epoch 4-5, indicating miscalibration that could corrupt the reweighting step.

List_of_included_plots.description: Mean authenticity score per week (control vs treatment): Control remains essentially constant at ~1.0 with negligible variance, while treatment declines after week ~3 from ~1.0 to ~0.4 by week 7, with large and increasing variance (shaded band widening dramatically).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This is the clearest empirical signal across all experiments: high-AI-exposure users show a marked decline in self-reported authenticity beginning around week 4, while control users remain at ceiling (~1.0). The abrupt divergence around week 3-4 suggests a cumulative exposure threshold effect rather than a linear relationship. The widening variance in the treatment group by week 7 is consistent with heterogeneous individual susceptibility to AI-induced drift. This plot directly supports the core hypothesis that AI interaction induces measurable value drift.

List_of_included_plots.description: Three-panel summary (drift detector loss, PDAS, reward model loss): Drift detector shows classic overfitting: training loss decreases steadily (~0.5 to ~0.07) while validation loss bottoms early (~0.18-0.2 around epoch 4-5) then increases to ~0.38 by epoch 30.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The overfitting of the drift detector (train loss 0.069 vs val loss 0.386 at epoch 30) is a critical failure mode for Implementation B. Because detector probabilities are used as recalibration weights, miscalibrated (overconfident) predictions on non-training data will lead to incorrect down-weighting. The simultaneous improvement in PDAS and reward model loss despite detector overfitting suggests these metrics may be partially driven by training-set patterns rather than generalizable causal attribution. Early stopping at the validation loss minimum (~epoch 4-5) is strongly indicated.

List_of_included_plots.description: PDAS over training epochs: The corrected PDAS drops sharply from ~1.2 at epoch 1 to ~0.65 by ~epoch 7-8, then gradually to ~0.52-0.58 with small oscillations through epoch 30. The dashed baseline 'Raw PDAS = 1.221' indicates the uncorrected drift metric.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The corrected PDAS of ~0.527 vs. raw PDAS of 1.221 (a 56.8% reduction) demonstrates that Implementation B's recalibration pipeline substantially reduces measured AI-induced drift. The steep early decline followed by a plateau suggests the model quickly learns the main correction signal but cannot eliminate all residual drift, possibly due to irreducible noise or detector overfitting limiting further improvement. The oscillations after epoch 10 are consistent with optimization noise from a miscalibrated detector.

List_of_included_plots.description: Drift Attribution: AI-Induced vs Authentic Evolution (bar chart over timesteps 1-7). Clear monotonic increase in AI-induced drift magnitude across timesteps (roughly ~0.045 to ~0.22). Authentic drift remains an order of magnitude smaller throughout (roughly ~0.002-0.012).
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This is the key causal attribution result from Implementation A. The monotonic accumulation of AI-induced drift (0.045 to 0.217 across 7 timesteps) while authentic drift stays near-zero (0.002-0.012) demonstrates that the counterfactual comparison method cleanly separates the two sources. The 27x magnitude difference at the final timestep (AI-induced: 0.217 vs authentic: 0.008) provides strong quantitative evidence for the drift hypothesis. The temporal accumulation pattern is consistent with a compounding feedback loop, which is the mechanistic story motivating this research.

List_of_included_plots.description: Longitudinal Preference Trajectories by AI Interaction Level. High AI interaction users: mean trajectory increases steadily from ~0.01 at t=0 to ~0.15 at t=7. Low AI interaction users: mean remains nearly flat (~0.015-0.02) across timesteps.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The difference-in-differences pattern is clearly visible: high-AI users show systematic upward drift in preferences while low-AI users remain stable. Both groups start near the same baseline, supporting the causal interpretation. The increasing variance in the high-AI group over time is consistent with heterogeneous treatment effects and suggests individual susceptibility modeling would be valuable. The presence of some high-AI users with negative drift (visible in individual trajectories) indicates the drift effect is not universal, which motivates latent variable modeling of susceptibility in future work.

List_of_included_plots.description: Preference Drift Attribution Score (PDAS) over Training. PDAS hovers high (~0.94-0.957) throughout training, with step-like changes. Mid-training dip to ~0.938-0.942 followed by recovery to final ~0.965.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The high and stable PDAS (~0.965 final) in Implementation A indicates that the simpler counterfactual approach reliably attributes drift to AI interaction throughout training. The decoupling between MSE loss (which decreases smoothly) and PDAS (which changes in steps and temporarily dips) is scientifically important: optimizing predictive accuracy does not automatically optimize causal attribution quality. This motivates treating PDAS as a separate evaluation criterion or auxiliary training objective.

List_of_included_plots.description: Authenticity score distribution at final week (week 8): The histogram appears dominated by the treatment distribution with mass near 0-0.1 and another spike near ~0.95-1.0, suggesting a bimodal outcome.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The bimodal distribution of authenticity scores in the treatment group at week 8 is a key finding: a subset of high-AI-exposure users retain high authenticity (~1.0) while another subset drops to near-zero. This heterogeneity suggests that AI-induced drift is not uniform and that individual susceptibility factors (possibly baseline values, engagement patterns, or cognitive traits) modulate vulnerability. This motivates future work on susceptibility modeling and targeted interventions for high-risk users.

Key_numerical_results.description: Final PDAS (Preference Drift Attribution Score) in Implementation A (node 31598), indicating the fraction of observed preference change attributable to AI interaction
Key_numerical_results.analysis: A PDAS of 0.9645 means that ~96.5% of observed preference shifts in high-AI-interaction users are attributable to AI influence rather than authentic evolution. This high value validates the core hypothesis that AI interaction is the dominant driver of preference change in the synthetic experiment. Without correction, this level of contamination would severely bias RLHF reward models.

Key_numerical_results.description: Raw PDAS before recalibration in seed implementation (nodes 59eee/b601a/c761d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Consistent with node 31598's result (~0.9645), confirming that the baseline level of AI-induced drift contamination is approximately 96% of total observed preference change. This robustly establishes the severity of the drift problem in the synthetic setup.

Key_numerical_results.description: Corrected PDAS after recalibration in seed implementation (nodes 59eee/b601a/c761d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: After applying inverse-probability recalibration weights, PDAS drops from 0.9615 to 0.2925, a 69.6% reduction. This demonstrates that the recalibration step substantially reduces the measured AI-induced contamination, though ~29% residual drift remains, indicating the correction is incomplete.

Key_numerical_results.description: Percentage reduction in PDAS after recalibration (seed implementation)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: A 69.6% PDAS reduction is the primary evidence for the effectiveness of the drift-aware recalibration approach. However, the remaining 30.4% uncorrected drift suggests room for improvement, possibly through better drift detection, stronger regularization, or multi-step correction.

Key_numerical_results.description: Corrected PDAS in Implementation B (node 34d65) after reward model recalibration
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Implementation B achieves a corrected PDAS of 0.527 vs. raw PDAS of 1.221, a 56.8% reduction. This is a lower percentage reduction than the seed implementation (69.6%), possibly because Implementation B uses a different PDAS definition (not bounded to [0,1]) and suffers from drift detector overfitting that limits recalibration effectiveness.

Key_numerical_results.description: Raw PDAS in Implementation B (node 34d65) before correction
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The raw PDAS exceeding 1.0 indicates a different operational definition than the seed implementation (which uses a bounded ratio). Here PDAS likely measures absolute divergence magnitude rather than a ratio, making direct comparison across implementations non-trivial. Standardizing the PDAS definition is needed for cross-method comparison.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation MSE for preference prediction in Implementation A (node 31598)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Implementation A achieves very low validation MSE (0.0039), indicating the simpler neural network accurately captures preference trajectories. This contrasts sharply with Implementation B's validation loss of 0.386, suggesting the added complexity of the drift detector in Implementation B does not improve (and may harm) preference prediction quality.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation loss in seed/Implementation B nodes (59eee/b601a/c761d/34d65)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The much higher validation loss (0.355 vs 0.0039 for node 31598) in the more complex implementation suggests that adding the drift detection and recalibration components introduces optimization challenges and may reduce the quality of the underlying preference model.

Key_numerical_results.description: AI-induced drift magnitude at the final timestep (t=7) in Implementation A (node 31598)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The AI-induced drift of 0.217 at the last timestep is 27x larger than authentic drift (0.008), providing strong quantitative evidence for the dominance of AI-induced effects. The monotonic increase from ~0.045 at t=1 to 0.217 at t=7 confirms the compounding nature of AI-induced drift over time.

Key_numerical_results.description: Authentic (non-AI-induced) drift magnitude at the final timestep (t=7) in Implementation A (node 31598)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The near-zero authentic drift (0.008) throughout the experiment confirms that the counterfactual comparison method successfully isolates AI-induced changes. The 27:1 ratio of AI-induced to authentic drift at t=7 is the key quantitative finding supporting the drift hypothesis.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation accuracy of the drift detector in Implementation B (node 34d65)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: While 91.56% validation accuracy seems reasonable, the simultaneous rise in validation loss (from ~0.18 to ~0.386) indicates increasing miscalibration: the model becomes more confident in wrong predictions over training. Since detector probabilities are used as recalibration weights, miscalibration is more harmful than raw accuracy suggests. Early stopping at epoch 4-5 (val loss minimum) is recommended.

Key_numerical_results.description: Validation MSE for reward prediction in seed implementation (nodes 59eee/b601a/c761d)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The reward model achieves reasonable predictive accuracy (MSE=0.0831, RMSE=0.2883) on the recalibrated feedback. This indicates that the recalibration step does not catastrophically destabilize reward model training, and the down-weighted feedback still contains sufficient signal for preference learning.
